title: The Captcha Barrier
concept: Understanding why CAPTCHAs stop agents, and designing graceful failure pathways when they occur.

exercises.prompt: What happens to agents when they hit a login block or a rate limit page?

options.text: They can continue as usual without any issues.
They are stopped until conditions change.
They can bypass the block easily.

exercises.explain: When agents hit a login block or rate limit page, they are essentially stopped until the conditions that caused the block change.

cards.title: What is a CAPTCHA?
cards.body: CAPTCHA stands for "Completely Automated Public Turing test to tell Computers and Humans Apart." It's a challenge that helps websites know if a user is a real person or a bot.

cards.title: Why CAPTCHAs Exist
cards.body: CAPTCHAs exist to prevent automated agents from abusing web services, which helps keep websites secure and usable for people.

cards.title: CAPTCHAs as Security Guards
cards.body: Think of CAPTCHAs as security guards at a building. They check to make sure only authorized visitors (humans) can enter, keeping out unauthorized ones (bots).

cards.title: CAPTCHA Examples
cards.body: Common examples include typing words from a distorted image, solving simple math problems, or selecting certain images from a grid.

cards.title: Myth: CAPTCHAs are Always Easy to Solve
cards.body: Many people believe CAPTCHAs are just simple tests, but some can be very challenging even for humans, making them a solid barrier against bots.

cards.title: CAPTCHA Comparison
cards.body: Visual CAPTCHAs often require user interaction, while invisible ones don't show challenges unless they suspect a bot.

left.text: User types a code from an image.

exercises.prompt: What is a primary purpose of CAPTCHAs?

options.text: To identify humans and prevent bots.
To speed up the login process.

exercises.explain: The primary purpose of CAPTCHAs is to differentiate humans from bots to maintain web security.

exercises.prompt: CAPTCHAs can completely stop all bots from accessing websites.
exercises.explain: While CAPTCHAs are effective at stopping many bots, they cannot stop every single bot, particularly sophisticated ones.

exercises.prompt: CAPTCHAs are used to prevent _____ from accessing web services.
exercises.sentence: CAPTCHAs are used to prevent bots from accessing web services.
exercises.explain: CAPTCHAs are designed specifically to stop bots from abusing web services.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements about CAPTCHAs is generated by AI?

options.text: CAPTCHAs can both help secure websites and sometimes frustrate users.
All CAPTCHAs are easy for both bots and humans.
CAPTCHAs are only used on social media sites.

exercises.explain: The correct statement reminds us that while CAPTCHAs provide security, they can also cause frustration for users.

exercises.prompt: What should you consider designing when a CAPTCHA blocks an agent?

options.text: A way to notify users and give assistance to pass the CAPTCHA.
A way to make the CAPTCHA harder for everyone.
A method to hide the CAPTCHA completely.
A procedure to bypass the CAPTCHA altogether.

exercises.explain: It's important to design pathways that notify users when they hit a CAPTCHA and provide helpful guidance on how to pass it.
